name: HTTP TRACE method enabled — Cross-Site Tracing (XST) vector
description: Sends an HTTP TRACE request to the target root. Servers that echo the request back (RFC 7231 default behaviour) enable Cross-Site Tracing — a classic XST attack where a malicious cross-origin script triggers a TRACE from the victim's browser and reads the echo of HttpOnly cookies / Authorization headers the script normally can't touch. Modern browsers block fetch-driven TRACE since ~2007, but Flash/SilverlightActiveX-vintage clients and certain proxy chains still allow it; the better fix is server-side disable.

vulnerability.remediation: Disable the TRACE method at the server / load balancer. nginx: `if ($request_method = TRACE) { return 405; }` in the relevant location block. ASP.NET Core / Kestrel: TRACE is opt-in via routing; just don't map a TRACE handler (the default 404 is fine). IIS: <verbs allowUnlisted="false"> in <handlers>, or use the URL Rewrite module to reject TRACE. AWS ALB / CloudFront: WAF rule blocking the verb.
